mission.title: Install and Configure Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS)
mission.description: Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) is a managed Kubernetes service that simplifies the deployment, management, and operations of Kubernetes clusters in Azure. It is ideal for users looking to leverage Kubernetes without the overhead of managing the underlying infrastructure. Key trade-offs include reliance on Azure's infrastructure and potential costs associated with resource usage.

steps.title: Install Azure CLI
steps.description:
The Azure CLI is required to interact with Azure services, including AKS.

```bash

curl -sL https://aka.ms/InstallAzureCLIDeb | sudo bash

```

> This command installs the Azure CLI on Debian-based systems.

steps.title: Create an AKS Cluster
steps.description:
This command creates a new AKS cluster with the specified Kubernetes version, node count, and region.

```bash

az aks create --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --node-count 3 --kubernetes-version 1.31 --location eastus

```

> Replace 'myResourceGroup' and 'myAKSCluster' with your desired names.
commands: az aks create --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --node-count 3 --kubernetes-version 1.31 --location eastus

steps.title: Configure kubectl
steps.description:
This command configures kubectl to use the new AKS cluster by updating the kubeconfig file.

```bash

az aks get-credentials --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster

```

> This command merges the AKS cluster credentials into your kubeconfig.

steps.title: Verify Cluster Nodes
steps.description:
This command checks the status of the nodes in the AKS cluster to ensure they are running.

```bash

kubectl get nodes

```

> You should see the nodes listed with a status of 'Ready'.

steps.title: Post-Installation Configuration
steps.description:
Set up monitoring and autoscaling for your AKS cluster to ensure optimal performance.

```bash

az aks enable-addons --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --addons monitoring

az aks update --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --enable-cluster-autoscaler --min-count 1 --max-count 5

```

> This enables monitoring for the cluster.
> This enables cluster autoscaling.
commands: az aks enable-addons --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --addons monitoring

uninstall.title: Delete AKS Cluster
uninstall.description:
This command deletes the AKS cluster and all associated resources. Be aware that this will result in data loss and orphaned resources such as load balancers and PVCs.

```bash

az aks delete --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --yes --no-wait

```

uninstall.title: Remove Resource Group
uninstall.description:
This command deletes the entire resource group, which includes all resources created within it.

```bash

az group delete --name myResourceGroup --yes --no-wait

```

upgrade.title: Upgrade AKS Cluster
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your workloads and configurations. This command upgrades the AKS cluster to the specified version.

```bash

az aks upgrade --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --kubernetes-version 1.31

```

> Monitor the upgrade process for any issues.

upgrade.title: Verify Upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, verify that the nodes are running the new version.

```bash

kubectl get nodes

```

> Check that the nodes are updated to the new version.

upgrade.title: Post-Upgrade Configuration
upgrade.description:
Re-enable any necessary add-ons or configurations that may have been disabled during the upgrade.

```bash

az aks enable-addons --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --addons monitoring

```

> Re-enable monitoring if it was disabled.
commands: az aks enable-addons --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --addons monitoring

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create the AKS cluster'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur due to insufficient permissions or quota limits in your Azure subscription.

**Fix:**
Check your Azure subscription permissions and ensure you have enough quota for the resources being requested.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'kubectl get nodes' returns 'No resources found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the AKS cluster may not be properly configured or the nodes are not ready.

**Fix:**
Run 'az aks get-credentials' to ensure your kubeconfig is set up correctly, and check the AKS portal for node status.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient CPU resources available'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen if the requested node count exceeds the available resources in the selected region.

**Fix:**
Reduce the node count or choose a different region with available resources.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to enable monitoring'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the Azure Monitor service is not available in your region.

**Fix:**
Check the Azure Monitor availability in your region and try again.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new Kubernetes features such as PodSecurity admission and improved API server performance.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated the use of legacy network policies.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v1.30, ensure that your network policies are updated to use the new format.

resolution.summary: You should see the output of 'kubectl get nodes' displaying the nodes with a status of 'Ready'.
codeSnippets: az aks create --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --node-count 3 --kubernetes-version 1.31 --location eastus
az aks enable-addons --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --addons monitoring

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have the Azure CLI and kubectl installed and configured.
